risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were randomized to the treatment or control group in a 1 : 1 ratio and stratified on BMI category ... age ... and current antidepressant medication use ... Computer-generated random numbers were used by the site statistician at the UCSF General Clinical Research Center (GCRC) to assign group condition." Comment: The paper explicitly describes a proper random sequence generation method (computer-generated random numbers), with stratification variables specified and assignment conducted by a statistician, which supports truly random allocation.

Quote: "Computer-generated random numbers were used by the site statistician at the UCSF General Clinical Research Center (GCRC) to assign group condition. After all participants had completed baseline assessments, this information was given to study staff who informed participants of their group condition." Comment: Although the sequence generation itself was computerized, the key concealment detail is that assignments were held by the site statistician and only released to study staff after baseline assessments were completed. This indicates the enrolling/recruiting staff did not have access to upcoming allocations before participant enrolment/baseline completion, so foreknowledge of the next assignment was prevented.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were not blinded to study hypotheses." and "After all participants had completed baseline assessments, this information was given to study staff who informed participants of their group condition." Comment: This was a waitlist-controlled behavioral intervention, so participants and personnel delivering/informing about the intervention knew group assignment. The study also included outcomes plausibly influenced by such knowledge, including multiple self-reported psychological and eating-behavior measures (e.g. perceived stress, anxiety, mindfulness, emotional/external eating). The paper itself notes this concern: "participants were unblinded to the hypotheses of the study about stress and abdominal fat, which could affect behavior in both groups." Therefore performance bias is at high risk overall.

Quote: "Participants were not blinded to study hypotheses." and "After all participants had completed baseline assessments, this information was given to study staff who informed participants of their group condition." and "Study nurses, blind to participant condition, performed the anthropometric and body composition assessments and blood draws. Research assistants administered the computerized questionnaires and provided instructions for the home saliva sampling procedure, but were not blind to participant condition at posttreatment assessments." Comment: Outcomes were assessed by a mix of patient self-report (mindfulness, stress, anxiety, eating behavior questionnaires), blinded nurses for anthropometric/body composition/blood draws, and unblinded research assistants for questionnaire administration. Because the study included subjective self-reported outcomes and participants knew their group/hypotheses, the patient was effectively an unblinded outcome assessor for those outcomes. Under the worst-outcome rule, these unblinded subjective outcomes make detection bias high overall, even though some biological and body-composition outcomes were measured by blinded/objective methods.

Quote: "Five treatment and two control participants were lost to followup for the primary analysis (see Figure 1). One control participant received liposuction and was included in the intention-to-treat analyses but was excluded from treatment efficacy and secondary analyses involving any biological outcomes." Quote: "Assuming participants lost to followup did not change over time, missing data at postintervention were imputed using preintervention values." Quote: Table 2 shows intention-to-treat N values varying by outcome, e.g. "Mindfulness-Aware ... N (t,c) 24, 22" and "Chronic stress ... N (t,c) 24, 23." Comment: Post-randomization loss is reported as 5/24 (20.8%) in treatment vs 2/23 (8.7%) in control for the primary analysis, a differential of about 12 percentage points, which could indicate high attrition bias. However, the paper does not provide enough outcome-specific analysed denominators/reasons across the outcomes of interest, and Table 2 suggests different denominators by outcome with ITT imputation for some measures. Reasons for loss to follow-up are not reported per arm, and biological secondary analyses additionally exclude at least one control participant. Because the outcome-specific attrition and exclusions cannot be fully established from the report, this domain is best judged unclear rather than high.
